title: L04 · Articles, Gender, Plural
objective: German nouns have three genders (masculine, feminine, neuter). You MUST learn each noun together with its article. This lesson gives you patterns + the most common 30 nouns.
strategy: Don't try to memorise the gender of every noun by rule — there are too many exceptions. Instead, learn each new noun WITH its article (say 'der Tisch', not just 'Tisch'). The patterns in section 2 help you guess about 70% of the time.

sections.title: The article system
sections.note: Two types of articles: DEFINITE (the) and INDEFINITE (a/an). Each has three gender forms in the nominative.

blocks.audioText: der Mann, die Frau, das Kind
blocks.rule: Three forms for 'the'. The form depends on the noun's grammatical gender, which has only a loose connection to natural gender. Plural is always 'die' regardless of gender.

examples.translation: the child (neut.) / el niño / la niña

blocks.audioText: ein Mann, eine Frau, ein Kind
blocks.rule: Two forms for 'a/an': 'ein' for masculine and neuter, 'eine' for feminine. There is NO indefinite plural — drop the article (English 'cars', not 'a cars').

examples.translation: Men work. (no article for plural indefinite) / Los hombres trabajan.

sections.title: Patterns that help you GUESS the gender
sections.note: Not 100% reliable but get you to ~70%. Memorise the suffixes — they're worth their weight in gold.

blocks.audioText: der Lehrer, der Student, der Doktor, der Montag
blocks.rule: Most nouns ending -er describing a doer are masculine. Days, months, seasons, points of the compass: also masculine.

blocks.audioText: die Lampe, die Wohnung, die Freiheit, die Nation, die Musik
blocks.rule: These suffixes are nearly ALWAYS feminine. Memorise them as a set — they cover thousands of nouns.

blocks.text: Neuter endings: -chen, -lein (diminutives), -ment, -um, -o, infinitives used as nouns
blocks.audioText: das Mädchen, das Dokument, das Zentrum, das Auto, das Essen
blocks.rule: Anything with -chen or -lein is automatically neuter — even das MÄDCHEN (girl), because '-chen' wins over natural gender. Verbs used as nouns are always neuter.

examples.translation: the eating, the food / la comida (verb 'essen' as noun)

sections.title: Plural patterns
sections.note: German plurals are not one-size-fits-all like English '-s'. There are FIVE main patterns. You'll learn each plural with its noun — but knowing the patterns helps you GUESS.

blocks.text: Plural pattern: + -er (often with Umlaut). Common for short neuter nouns.
blocks.audioText: das Kind → die Kinder, das Haus → die Häuser, das Buch → die Bücher
blocks.rule: If the noun is one syllable + neuter, the plural often adds -er and umlauts the vowel.

blocks.text: Plural pattern: + -(e)n. Most feminine nouns, plus many weak masculines.
blocks.audioText: die Frau → die Frauen, die Lampe → die Lampen, der Student → die Studenten
blocks.rule: If a noun ends in -e or -ung, -heit, -keit (all feminine), plural is almost always -n or -en.

blocks.text: Plural pattern: + -e (often with Umlaut). Common for many masculine nouns.
blocks.audioText: der Hund → die Hunde, der Stuhl → die Stühle, der Tag → die Tage
blocks.rule: Most one-syllable masculine nouns add -e in plural; if they have a vowel that CAN umlaut (a/o/u/au), it usually does.

blocks.text: Plural pattern: + -s. Mostly loanwords from English and French.
blocks.audioText: das Auto → die Autos, das Hotel → die Hotels, das Foto → die Fotos
blocks.rule: Recognisable foreign words usually take -s in plural.

blocks.text: Plural pattern: no change (or just Umlaut). Many nouns ending in -er, -el, -en.
blocks.audioText: der Lehrer → die Lehrer, der Apfel → die Äpfel
blocks.rule: Nouns ending in -er, -el, -en often have the SAME form in plural — only the article changes (der → die). Some umlaut.

sections.title: Articles in real sentences
sections.note: Listen and notice how articles signal who is doing what.

blocks.translation: The man is reading a book. / El hombre lee un libro.
The woman drinks a coffee. / La mujer toma un café.
blocks.hint: 'einen' (not 'ein') — because 'Kaffee' is the object (accusative). You'll learn this in L06.

blocks.translation: The child plays in the garden. / El niño juega en el jardín.
The teachers are nice. / Los profesores son simpáticos.
blocks.hint: 'die Lehrer' is plural here — same word as singular, but article 'die' and plural verb 'sind' make it clear.

blocks.text: Eine Frau und ein Mann gehen ins Café.
blocks.audioText: Eine Frau und ein Mann gehen ins Café.
blocks.translation: A woman and a man go to the café. / Una mujer y un hombre van al café.
